hero.h1: A payment routing simulator for merchant teams building new PSP integrations
hero.sub: Most PSP sandboxes produce too clean outputs to test real routing logic. This simulator helps returns the kinds of outcomes merchants actually see in production: realistic soft vs hard declines, 3DS behavior, latency spikes, cross-border BIN penalties etc.

section_what.label: What it is
section_what.heading: A realistic test environment for merchant integration and routing logic
section_what.body: The Payment Routing Simulator emulates how different processor types behave when a merchant sends them card transactions. Instead of always returning clean sandbox approvals, it models approval rates, decline codes, latency, 3DS behavior, cross-border gaps, and retry outcomes. Merchant can simulate the behaviour by  across five processor archetypes

section_why.label: Why it matters
section_why.heading: Routing decisions are usually tested in environments that do not reflect production
points: Sandbox behavior that provider models is often too optimistic, and all too often the edge cases appear only when the integration is in production and customers are seeing payment failures. Real mix of soft declines, issuer outages, latency spikes, authentication challenges are examples of such issues which can be tested with the simulator

section_who.label: Who it's for
section_who.heading: Merchant integration teams, QA engineers, solutions teams, and product stakeholders
section_who.body: Primary user: a merchant-side engineer or QA lead who needs to validate routing behavior before launch. Secondary users: PSP solutions engineers demonstrating realistic flows, product managers evaluating routing strategies, and data teams generating synthetic payment outcomes for analytics or ML experiments.

section_how_to_use.label: Try it yourself
section_how_to_use.heading: Two ways to hit the live API
section_how_to_use.option_1_title: Option 1 — rank every acquirer for this transaction profile
section_how_to_use.option_1_desc: Use /compare when you want every acquirer ranked against the same transaction profile. Returns each archetype with expected approval rate, latency, top decline code, and fee
section_how_to_use.option_2_title: Option 2 — test one specific acquirer archetype
section_how_to_use.option_2_desc: Use /simulate when you want to test a single provider by name. The full list of 11 provider names is returned by GET /providers

section_case_study.label: Re-run the transaction
section_case_study.heading: Re-run a transaction through different routing strategies
section_case_study.body: A built-in replay flow lets teams run the same transaction list through different routing setups and compare what changes: approvals, latency, retry salvage, and fee impact. That makes the simulator useful not just for individual transaction tests, but also for explaining the business effect of a routing policy to non-engineering stakeholders.

section_howbuilt.label: Stack
section_howbuilt.heading: An API-first simulator with state, retries, and event delivery
section_howbuilt.body_part1: Under the hood, the simulator is built as a FastAPI service backed by a payment state model, idempotency handling, webhook delivery, and processor profile logic. The goal is not to reproduce every production detail of a PSP, but to reproduce the parts that matter most when teams are testing routing logic and downstream payment handling.
section_howbuilt.body_part2: It also connects naturally to the wider portfolio: the chatbot can use it as a routing intelligence backend, and the same synthetic payment world can later feed replay analysis and recovery-model training.

right_panel.title: Compare archetypes
right_panel.subtitle: Pick a comparison to see the request and the ranked response the live /router/compare endpoint returns

chips.request: Spanish Visa prepaid at €45 with EU SCA exemption
Idempotency key hit on duplicate /simulate within 3s
US Visa $1,200 corporate card with Level-3 line-item data

0.recommendation: regional-bank-processor — issuer proximity wins for MX domestic debit

1.recommendation: regional-card-specialist — LVP exemption avoids 3DS challenge under €30; domestic routing keeps interchange in-region

2.recommendation: cross-border-fx-specialist — 30 bps lower FX markup wins once corridor volume > $10k/month

3.recommendation: high-risk-or-orchestrator — challenge frictionless via TRA on $2,500 is borderline; expect 8% fallback to OTP

4.salvage: Second-leg authorization on the regional-bank-processor archetype; total round-trip 327 ms, one extra webhook, net approved

5.note: Duplicate request returned cached response; no state transition, no Kafka event, no duplicate webhook

6.recommendation: L3 capture saves 50 bps of interchange; confirm all line items pass edit checks to avoid downgrade
